title: A resolution supporting the goals and ideals of World Malaria Day.
plain_summary: This bill is about supporting World Malaria Day, which is a special day to raise awareness about malaria. Malaria is a dangerous disease that can be prevented and treated, but it still kills many people around the world. The Senate wants to recognize the good progress that has been made in fighting malaria and reducing the number of people who get sick or die from it. The bill also says that when America helps other countries fight malaria, it makes our own country safer and stronger too.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

sections.title: Resolution Supporting the Goals and Ideals of World Malaria Day
sections.text: The Senate supports World Malaria Day, which happens every year on April twenty-fifth. This resolution explains why fighting malaria around the world helps the United States and recognizes the progress that has been made. Malaria is a disease that kills many people, but it can be prevented and treated. In two thousand twenty-three, there were about two hundred sixty-three million cases of malaria in eighty-three countries and five hundred ninety-seven thousand people died from it. Most of these cases happened in Africa. Children under five years old and pregnant women are hurt the most by malaria. Since two thousand, global efforts to fight malaria have prevented about two billion two hundred million cases and saved about twelve million seven hundred thousand lives. The United States has been a leader in this fight through programs like the President's Malaria Initiative and by giving money to the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria. Forty-five countries and one territory have gotten rid of malaria completely. Some countries like Rwanda, India, and Liberia have made great progress in reducing cases. There are new tools to fight malaria including better bed nets, medicines, vaccines for children, bug sprays, and new technologies. However, progress has slowed down because bugs are becoming resistant to sprays and medicines, and because of wars and other problems. About two thousand Americans get malaria each year when they travel to places where the disease exists. Fighting malaria helps keep Americans safe when they travel or work overseas. It also prevents malaria from coming back to the United States, which happened in Florida, Texas, and Maryland in two thousand twenty-three. Fighting malaria makes America stronger by helping create stable governments around the world and competing with China's influence in important regions. It also makes America more prosperous by supporting American research, universities, and businesses. One study found that American exports could grow by one billion four hundred eighty million dollars if the world reaches its goals to reduce malaria by two thousand thirty. It costs much less to prevent malaria than to treat it. Preventing one person from getting malaria costs between fifty-three cents and five dollars ninety-seven cents, while treating severe malaria costs about one hundred forty-five dollars twenty-three cents. The Senate supports the goals of World Malaria Day and finds that fighting malaria helps America's national interests. The Senate recognizes how important it is to reduce malaria to improve the health of mothers and children. The Senate praises the progress made through American programs and supports efforts to reduce malaria cases and deaths by at least ninety percent by two thousand thirty. The Senate supports countries that are working to prevent and treat malaria and encourages them to eventually take full control of their own malaria programs. The Senate welcomes partnerships between government and private companies to create better and cheaper tools to fight malaria. The Senate supports continued American leadership in reducing malaria around the world through government programs and partnerships with other countries and private organizations.
